perturbation.original_text: Customer shall exercise its option, if at all, by delivering written notice to M&I at least five (5) months prior to expiration of the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer shall exercise its option, if at all, by delivering written notice to M&I at least three (3) months prior to expiration of the Term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for exercising the renewal option could be problematic under general contract law principles if it's deemed an unreasonably short time, especially considering the complexity of the services. This may not directly contradict a specific statute but may make the clause unenforceable under general principles of contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309(3) states that termination of a contract requires reasonable notification, and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. While this isn't a termination clause, the renewal option notification period could be argued as unreasonably short, making the agreement unconscionable, depending on the specific facts and circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period from five months to three months. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this reduction could be deemed an unreasonably short time, potentially making the agreement unconscionable under UCC § 2-309(3) if it violates the requirement for reasonable notification, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that 'Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable.'

perturbation.original_text: No lawsuit or other action may be brought by either party hereto, or on any claim or controversy based upon or arising in any way out of this Agreement be brought, after one (1) year from the date on which the cause of action arose; provided, however, the foregoing limitation shall not apply to the collection of any amounts due under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: No lawsuit or other action may be brought by either party hereto, or on any claim or controversy based upon or arising in any way out of this Agreement be brought, after six (6) months from the date on which the cause of action arose; provided, however, the foregoing limitation shall not apply to the collection of any amounts due under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations to six months may be unenforceable, as it may be considered unreasonably short, particularly for complex commercial disputes. Some jurisdictions have minimum statutes of limitations for contract claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: WI ST § 893.43 states that an action upon any contract shall be commenced within 6 years after the cause of action accrues or be barred. By reducing the time to 6 months, the clause is now illegal in the state of Wisconsin.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the statute of limitations for bringing a lawsuit from one year to six months, which directly contradicts the Law Explanation's assertion that WI ST § 893.43 requires actions upon a contract to be commenced within 6 years. This creates a clear and direct violation of the stated law, making the modified clause unenforceable in Wisconsin. The perturbation type, inconsistencies, aligns with this by changing a time-sensitive obligation that violates state law, falling under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.
